references.notes: This verse contains a pre-Islamic oath formula invoking the life of the father, a common practice in ancient Arabia.
The phrase 'ورب بقيع' is an oath formula invoking the 'lord of a specific place,' indicating a local sacred entity or a general concept of a lord.
This verse includes an explicit oath to 'your Lord,' referring to a deity in a pre-Islamic context.
The mention of 'الجني' (Jinn) explicitly refers to a class of supernatural beings central to pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The reference to 'تميمة' (amulet) signifies a pre-Islamic practice of wearing charms for protection, which falls under the physical dimensions of religious practice.
The term 'الإله' (The God) refers to a deity in a general sense, consistent with pre-Islamic concepts of a supreme being.
The phrase "لعمري" (by my life) is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, where one swears by their own life as a sacred entity.
The term "محراب" (mihrab) in a pre-Islamic context refers to a sacred niche or place of worship, indicating a sacred space.
This verse explicitly mentions "الجن" (jinn) as supernatural entities, performing actions around a sacred space.
The poet explicitly states sending a "جني" (jinn) to gather information, highlighting the belief in and interaction with supernatural beings.
The "شحيج غرابها" (croaking of a raven/crow) is a traditional pre-Islamic omen, used for augury.
"أنصاب" (standing stones or idols) were sacred objects of worship in pre-Islamic Arabia, and "عكف" (devotion/veneration) describes a ritual act performed around them.
The verse mentions the 'Ka'ba' of Raymān being ruined, referring to a pre-Islamic sacred cubical structure or sanctuary.
The term "شُعُوب" (shu'ūb) in pre-Islamic poetry often refers to death or the paths leading to it, indicating the ultimate fate of the enemies.
The phrase 'لعمرك' (By your life) is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, invoking the life of the addressee as a sacred entity.
The term 'al-maniyya' refers to fate or destiny, often conceived as a powerful, impersonal cosmological force in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase 'laʿamruka' is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, swearing by one's life or the life of the addressee.
This verse explicitly mentions "لأمي والقداح," which are types of arrows used for divination in pre-Islamic Arabian practices.
The verse explicitly mentions augury from birds (ṭīra) and the raven as an omen, which are practices of divination.
The mention of 'companions of Quzaḥ' refers to a pre-Islamic sacred figure associated with rituals at Muzdalifa.
The verses describe 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a treacherous and powerful force that causes change and misfortune, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate.
The verse explicitly prohibits performing rituals ('tanskunahu') to 'al-Naṣb al-Manṣūb' (standing stones/altars), which were sacred objects in pre-Islamic Arabian religion.
The verse explicitly prohibits the worship of 'al-Awthān' (idols), which were representations of deities in pre-Islamic polytheism.
The prohibition 'lā taḥmad al-Shayṭān' (do not praise Satan) refers to Shayṭān as a malevolent supernatural entity, consistent with pre-Islamic beliefs about jinn and evil spirits.
This verse contains an oath formula ('لعمر') invoking a sacred entity ('الذي حجت قريش قطينه') to whom Quraysh performed pilgrimage, referring to the Kaaba and its associated deity.
The phrase 'طاف طوفه' explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic ritual of circumambulation (ṭawāf) around a sacred object or space.
This verse invokes 'Allah' as a witness, which in pre-Islamic poetry often refers to the supreme deity in an oath-like context.
The verse explicitly mentions 'al-qurbān' (the offering/sacrifice), referring to a ritual act of presenting an offering, which is a key pre-Islamic religious practice.

The verse attributes agency to 'al-dahr' (time/fate), depicting it as a force that brings about change and corruption, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological belief in Dahr as an impersonal, governing power.
The verse refers to 'my lord' (ربي) who is invoked using 'parchments' (مهارق), indicating a pre-Islamic practice of invoking a personal or tribal deity through specific ritualistic means.
This verse contains an explicit oath formula, 'by God's oath' (يمين الله), which was a common pre-Islamic practice for swearing by a divine entity.
The verse attributes the provision of sustenance (رزقا) to 'The God' (الإله), reflecting a pre-Islamic belief in a divine provider.
This verse features an oath sworn 'by the life of your grandfather' (لعمر جدك), a common pre-Islamic oath formula invoking the sacredness of an ancestor or lineage.
This verse uses the phrase 'ḥumma li-mīʿādihā' (it was destined for its appointment), which refers to a preordained fate or destiny, a common cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
These verses describe the stars, specifically al-Thurayyā and al-Suʿūd, their movement, and their perceived enduring nature, reflecting pre-Islamic astral belief and cosmological understanding.

The phrase 'الشهر الحرام' (the sacred month) refers to the pre-Islamic Arabian practice of designating certain months as sacred, during which fighting was forbidden, indicating a ritual concept of sacred time.
The phrase "اذبح هديك" explicitly refers to the act of sacrificing an animal, which is a pre-Islamic ritual practice.
The term "المنايا" (fates/deaths) refers to an irresistible force of fate or death, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term "الدهر" (dahr/time) is invoked as a cosmological force, often associated with fate and destruction in pre-Islamic belief.
The verse explicitly mentions an "oath" (حلفة) and refers to "his great god" (لاهه الكبار) who hears it, indicating a pre-Islamic oath-taking practice involving a deity.
The verse mentions 'Abū Yaʿfūr', who is widely recognized as the jinn familiar or inspiring spirit of the poet al-Aʿshā, thus referencing a supernatural being.
This verse contains an oath 'by the House of God' (وبيت الله), which refers to the Kaaba, a central pre-Islamic sanctuary, making it an explicit reference to a sacred entity used in an oath formula.
The phrase "حليف زاره" refers to being an ally or confederate of Zāra, a pre-Islamic sacred entity or idol, indicating a form of dedication or veneration.
The term 'haykal' refers to a temple, shrine, or idol, indicating a sacred space or object central to pre-Islamic religious practice.
The phrase 'نذرت النذورا' (made vows/pledges) refers to the pre-Islamic practice of making votive offerings or pledges, often to deities or sacred places, which falls under ritualistic acts.
These verses describe the beloved's power as so great that she could bring the dead back to life, touching on the pre-Islamic understanding of death and the possibility of resurrection.
The poet invokes 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a powerful, often indifferent, cosmological force that governs events, a common pre-Islamic belief.
The verse explicitly mentions swearing an oath by Allāh, which was a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia, recognizing Allāh as a high god.
The term 'nādhir' refers to someone who makes a votive vow, which was a significant ritual practice in pre-Islamic Arabian religion.
The verse explicitly mentions 'al-kawāhin' (diviner-priestesses), referring to a pre-Islamic sacral role.
The term 'حج' (hajj) explicitly refers to a pre-Islamic ritual journey or pilgrimage, fitting the category of ritual practice.
The verse refers to "الدهر" (Time/Fate) as an active, destructive force, which aligns with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about dahr.
This verse again attributes agency to "دهر" (Time/Fate) in separating what was gathered, reflecting pre-Islamic cosmological views.
The mention of a "كتف" (shoulder blade) in someone's hand suggests the practice of scapulimancy, a form of divination known in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The hoot of the owl ("نئيم البوم") was often considered an ill omen or associated with death in pre-Islamic Arabian beliefs.
The verse explicitly mentions "الشيطين" (shayāṭīn), which are non-divine supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
"المنية" (Fate/Death) is presented as an active agent, similar to dahr, reflecting pre-Islamic cosmological concepts of an inescapable destiny.
The phrase "يستسقى الغمام به" (rain is sought through him) indicates a belief in the sacral role of a prominent figure to mediate with the divine for rain, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The phrase "وبيت الله" is an oath by the Kaaba, which was a central sacred sanctuary in pre-Islamic Arabia, making it an explicit reference to pre-Islamic religious practice.
The verse refers to "دهره المضلل" (his misleading dahr), which is a direct reference to the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of dahr as an impersonal force of fate or time.
The verse refers to "al-Dahr" (Time/Fate) as a powerful, governing force, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological views where dahr was often seen as an impersonal agent of destiny.
The verse personifies "al-Manīya" (Fate/Death) as a speaking entity that dictates human affairs, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of destiny.
The verse attributes a state of distress or madness to a "visitation of jinn," reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in jinn as supernatural entities capable of influencing humans.
The poet refers to "Misḥal" as the entity that inspires his speech, explicitly invoking the pre-Islamic concept of a poet's jinn familiar.
The verse explicitly states a partnership between the poet and a "jinn," further detailing the pre-Islamic belief in jinn as sources of poetic inspiration.
The verse contains an oath formula "تحالفا بأسحم داج," which can be interpreted as swearing by a dark, sacred entity or the darkness of night, a common practice in pre-Islamic oaths.
The term "محارم" (maḥārim) refers to sacred prohibitions or inviolable things/places, indicating a pre-Islamic concept of tribal taboos or sacred boundaries.
These verses explicitly mention a 'mārid from the misguided jinn' guarding the beloved, which is a direct reference to pre-Islamic supernatural entities.
The verses describe the use of 'ancient arrows/lots' (القداح العتاق) and 'the lot' (القدح) for a purpose that implies divination, a common pre-Islamic practice.
The term 'المحراب' (mihrab) in a pre-Islamic context often referred to a sacred chamber or a place of worship, indicating a ritual space.
This verse explicitly mentions birds informing people of an event, which is a clear reference to the pre-Islamic practice of augury or divination by birds (ṭīra).
The verse refers to 'al-dahr' (time/fate) as an irresistible force, aligning with the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of dahr as an impersonal agent of destiny.
This is an explicit oath made by invoking 'the Lord of those who prostrate,' a common pre-Islamic formula for swearing by a high god.
The term 'manāyā' (deaths/fates) is used here to refer to the irresistible force of death, a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verse explicitly mentions 'ghūl' (ghoul), a type of malevolent jinn or supernatural entity in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This verse explicitly mentions 'قرابين' (sacrifices/offerings), which refers to a central pre-Islamic ritual practice.
The phrase 'أمك هابل' is an imprecation or curse invoking Hubal, a prominent pre-Islamic deity.
The phrase 'ريب المنون' refers to the vicissitudes of fate or time, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept where fate is seen as an irresistible force.
This verse explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic practice of bird augury (ṭīra), where the appearance of a grey bird (شهباء) is feared for its ill omen (فال).
The term "الدهر" is used here as an impersonal cosmological force, representing fate or time, which is a significant pre-Islamic belief.
The verse mentions fearing a 'fāl' (omen) associated with the birth of a mare, which is an explicit reference to pre-Islamic belief in augury.
This verse explicitly mentions 'الجن' (jinn), which are non-divine supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.

references.entity_or_term: لعمر الذي خطت مناسمها له وسيق إليه الباقر الغيل
references.notes: This verse contains an oath formula 'لعمر' (by the life of) directed towards an unnamed sacred entity to whom sacrificial animals (wild cattle) are driven, indicating a pre-Islamic religious oath.
This verse refers to 'أيسار', the arrows or lots used in the pre-Islamic practice of maysir, which involved divination and distribution of camel meat.
The term 'زاجرا' refers to an augur, a person who practices divination, fitting the category of tribal sacral roles.
The phrase 'فلعمر' is a traditional pre-Islamic oath formula, invoking a higher power or entity.
This verse refers to a 'خالقها المليك' (sovereign creator) who decrees the 'حتف' (doom/death) of the soul, reflecting pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about fate and divine will.
The phrase 'دم الذبيح' (blood of the slaughtered) explicitly references animal sacrifice, a central ritual act in pre-Islamic Arabian religion, even though it is used here as a simile for wine.
The verse refers to the act of swearing an oath (أقسموا), which was a solemn and religiously significant practice in pre-Islamic Arabia, often involving invocation of deities or sacred entities.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) is used here to denote fate or time as a powerful, often indifferent or malevolent, cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic belief.

The phrase 'قيامهم للهلال' (their standing for the crescent moon) describes a ritualistic act of reverence or worship directed towards the crescent moon, a known celestial object of veneration in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The verse explicitly mentions 'shayāṭīn' (devils/jinn) in the context of intense love, reflecting pre-Islamic beliefs about jinn influencing human emotions and actions.
The verse contains an explicit oath sworn by 'Allah,' a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia where Allah was recognized as a high god.
The term 'حرام' (forbidden/sacred) carries strong pre-Islamic religious connotations, referring to something inviolable or prohibited by divine authority ('ربنا').
The verse contains an oath sworn by 'the Lord of the dancing women,' referring to a pre-Islamic deity or sacred entity, and mentions Minā, a sacred pilgrimage site.
The verse refers to sacred locations in Mecca, al-Ḥajūn, al-Ṣafā, and the well of Zamzam, which were significant in pre-Islamic pilgrimage and ritual.
The verse mentions 'al-Raḥmān' in connection with 'your house' (بيتك) and 'al-Muḥarram' (the sacred precinct), alluding to a sacred structure and its associated deity and sacred space.
The verse refers to 'Bayt Allāh' (the House of God), likely the Kaaba, as a sacred structure in pre-Islamic belief.
The verse mentions 'mawsam' (seasonal gathering), which in pre-Islamic Arabia often had religious significance and was associated with rituals and fairs.

references.entity_or_term: التي بناها قصي والمضاض بن جرهم
references.notes: The verse explicitly refers to the Kaaba, built by Quṣayy and the Jurhum tribe, as a central pre-Islamic sanctuary.
The verse explicitly mentions 'my jinn brother,' referring to a jinn familiar believed to inspire poets in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse refers to 'birds of ill omen,' a practice of augury or divination from birds common in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.

The verse describes a guardian performing ritualistic actions of slaughter ('dhubiḥat'), prayer/blessing ('ṣallā'), and muttering/chanting ('zamzamā') over an offering, which are indicative of pre-Islamic sacrificial practices.
The phrase 'يد الدهر' (the hand of Time/Fate) explicitly refers to 'dahr' as an active, controlling cosmological force, a central concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This phrase refers to the inexorable cycles of time and fate, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept where time (dahr) is a powerful, often destructive, agent.
The poet swears an oath by 'the head of the spring/eye,' which represents a sacred entity or place, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabian oaths.
These terms refer to death and fate as an inescapable, preordained end, reflecting pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about destiny.
The 'Saʿālī' are a type of female jinn or ghoul in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore, explicitly referencing supernatural beings.
The verse explicitly mentions 'jinn' inhabiting a desolate place, which aligns with pre-Islamic beliefs about these supernatural entities.
The phrase 'circumambulated the rujma' refers to the ritual practice of circumambulating sacred stone piles or cairns, a known pre-Islamic Arabian custom.
The phrase "حل حراما" refers to violating a sacred prohibition or an inviolable sacred space, a concept central to pre-Islamic Arabian ritual and law.
This verse explicitly mentions "al-qidāḥ" (divination arrows), which were used in pre-Islamic Arabian divination practices.
The term 'المنون' (al-Manūn) refers to fate or death as an impersonal, destructive force, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term 'الدهر' (al-Dahr) is invoked as an active, often destructive, force of time and fate, a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term 'الزمن' (al-Zaman) is used here synonymously with 'الدهر' as an active, often treacherous, force of time and fate, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological view.
The phrase 'بيت الوثن' (house of idols) explicitly refers to a pre-Islamic sacred space, and 'طوف' (circumambulation) describes a ritual practice associated with such places.
The term 'Il refers to an ancient Semitic deity, indicating a reference to a pre-Islamic divine entity.
This verse contains an explicit oath sworn by the father, a common pre-Islamic oath formula invoking an ancestor as a sacred entity.
The term "المنى" refers to fate or destiny, a central cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief, often seen as an inescapable force.
This verse contains an oath sworn by "the piercing stars," indicating the invocation of celestial bodies as sacred entities in a pre-Islamic oath formula.
The term "منيته" refers to one's appointed death or fate, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of an inescapable destiny.
